parameters.root_cause: Deployment of payment-api v2.3.1 omitted a required environment variable (DB_CONNECTION_STRING), causing a NullPointerException in config.DatabaseConfig.getConnection (DatabaseConfig.java:42) at startup. The v2.3.1 deploy ~10:25:00Z corresponds with a spike to ~92% error rate and logs showing repeated "NullPointerException: DB_CONNECTION_STRING is null" and health check failures; payment-api reports 0 active DB connections while postgres-primary remains healthy (42 active connections). This is a configuration regression in the payment-api deployment (v2.3.1) resulting in HTTP 500s for customer checkouts.
parameters.recommendation: Immediate remediation: 1) Roll back payment-api to the previous known-good release (v2.3.0) to restore traffic immediately (kubectl rollout undo deployment/payment-api --to-revision=<v2.3.0> or use deploy pipeline rollback). 2) Hotfix option: update the payment-api deployment manifest to set DB_CONNECTION_STRING from the correct secret/configmap (e.g. env: DB_CONNECTION_STRING: valueFrom: secretKeyRef: name: payment-db-credentials, key: connection_string) and redeploy payment-api v2.3.1 with the corrected env var; then verify pods restart and health checks pass. 3) Post-recovery verification: confirm payment-api error_rate <1%, p99 latency normal, and DB connections >0; check logs for successful DB connection and absence of NullPointerException. 4) Prevent recurrence: add a CI pre-deploy validation that required env vars (DB_CONNECTION_STRING) are present, add a readiness probe that verifies DB connectivity (fail ready if env missing), and change DatabaseConfig to validate env at startup and exit with a clear error code (IllegalStateException) instead of throwing an NPE so broken pods don't serve traffic. 5) Runbook actions: annotate the deployment that caused the incident (payment-api v2.3.1), document rollback steps, and schedule a postmortem to identify why the env var was omitted from the release manifest.

parameters.root_cause: Inventory-service v2.1.4 (deployed 5 days ago) is the root cause. Inventory is UNHEALTHY with 45% error rate, p99 latency reporting timeouts and 4 restarts in the last 30 minutes — symptoms consistent with a runtime crash/OOM or memory leak/regression introduced in v2.1.4. Inventory serves upstream calls from checkout-service; when inventory times out or crashes, checkout-service v3.8.0 accumulates waiting requests (checkout p99 = 5.2s, error rate 12%), causing customer-facing latency. Inventory shows only 12 active DB connections (postgres) so DB connection pool exhaustion is unlikely; no other recent infra changes were detected, pointing to a service-level regression in inventory-service code or dependency in v2.1.4.
parameters.recommendation: Immediate mitigations (execute within next 10-30 minutes): 1) Roll back inventory-service from v2.1.4 to last known-good v2.1.3 immediately and perform a rolling restart. 2) Scale inventory replicas to 3 (if currently <3) to restore capacity while rollback completes. 3) If rollback cannot be performed within 10 minutes, increase inventory container memory limit by 50% (or +512Mi) and perform a rolling restart to relieve OOM/crash loops; check pod events for OOMKilled. 4) On checkout-service, implement a short client-side timeout and circuit breaker for inventory calls (e.g., 2s timeout, fail-fast) to prevent cascading latency and restore SLA for customers. 5) Post-mortem/diagnostics: collect inventory logs & JVM/GC/heap traces, check for OOMKilled events, inspect for long-running Postgres queries and kill queries >30s if present; consider setting statement_timeout=30s as a short-term DB guard. Long-term: identify offending change in v2.1.4, patch or revert the regression and add memory/cpu regression tests and early alerting for restart rates.

Short/medium-term fixes (hours to days):

2) Introduce connection pooling (PgBouncer) in front of postgres-primary, and tune pool sizes per service (set analytics-worker pool <= 10, auth-service pool sized for expected concurrency).
3) Reduce analytics-worker concurrency and add statement_timeout / query time limits for long-running analytical queries.
4) Add monitoring/alerts for DB connection usage and long-running queries, and add runbook steps to auto-throttle or pause heavy jobs.
Long-term (weeks):
1) Re-architect analytics to use ETL to a datawarehouse or read-replicas for large jobs, and enforce per-job resource quotas.
2) Capacity plan: right-size postgres instance and set safe defaults for max_connections in coordination with pooling.

parameters.root_cause: Root cause: Postgres connection exhaustion on postgres-primary (PostgreSQL 13.x). Timeline & evidence: notification-service v3.1.0 was deployed at 2026-04-01T14:02:00Z (feature: batch grouping, no schema changes). At 2026-04-01T14:10:00Z analytics-worker started the scheduled quarterly_event_analysis job which runs heavy analytical queries against postgres-primary (no read-replica configured). This job opened many long-running connections and executed heavy read queries, driving postgres_primary connection_count to the max and producing DB-level errors (connection slot exhaustion / “too many connections” and failures to accept new client connections). Upstream impact: auth-service (depends on postgres-primary) began failing to obtain DB connections (errors like “could not obtain connection from pool” / connection timeout), causing auth-service errors and cascading failures to user-profile-service and notification-service (api-gateway dependent services saw 5xx). Technical details observed: sustained high connections and long-running queries from analytics-worker, error spikes on auth-service coincident with DB connection saturation, no DB schema change on recent deploys. Likely root-cause is the analytics-worker job consuming all DB connections and exhausting postgres_primary max_connections (and/or insufficient pooling), not the notification-service deploy.
parameters.recommendation:
Immediate mitigation (next 5-15 minutes):
1) Pause/cancel the running analytics job: stop analytics-worker scheduled job or disable the quarterly_event_analysis run. If using a scheduler, cancel the job. (Command examples: stop analytics-worker process or scheduler job).
2) Cancel long-running DB queries from analytics-worker: on postgres-primary run: SELECT pid, usename, now()-query_start AS runtime, query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state='active' AND query_start < now() - interval '2 minutes'; then pg_cancel_backend(pid) for queries < graceful threshold, pg_terminate_backend(pid) for unresponsive ones.
3) If immediate cancellations fail and connections remain at max, temporarily increase max_connections (e.g., from current value to +100-200) and/or restart postgres during a brief maintenance window to clear stuck connections.
Short/medium-term fixes (hours to days):
1) Configure a read-replica for analytics workloads and update analytics-worker to target the replica for heavy reads (avoid hitting primary).
2) Introduce connection pooling (PgBouncer) in front of postgres-primary, and tune pool sizes per service (set analytics-worker pool <= 10, auth-service pool sized for expected concurrency).
3) Reduce analytics-worker concurrency and add statement_timeout / query time limits for long-running analytical queries.
4) Add monitoring/alerts for DB connection usage and long-running queries, and add runbook steps to auto-throttle or pause heavy jobs.
Long-term (weeks):
1) Re-architect analytics to use ETL to a datawarehouse or read-replicas for large jobs, and enforce per-job resource quotas.
2) Capacity plan: right-size postgres instance and set safe defaults for max_connections in coordination with pooling.
These steps will relieve immediate saturation (pause job + cancel queries), stop the cascade (auth-service errors), and prevent recurrence (pooling, replica, throttling).
